style: An illustrated storybook plate matching the Grants Pass chapter slideshows (ch35–ch38): hand-drawn black ink linework with soft watercolor washes on aged cream parchment. NOT a photograph, NOT photoreal, NOT 3D, NOT digital painting with smooth gradients — it is a comic/storybook illustration with visible ink outlines and watercolor texture. Outer margin is soft cream parchment with a faint aged-paper texture and a thin decorative rectangular frame; optional small green Art Nouveau corner flourishes. A short handwritten cursive caption in the lower corner. COLOR BEHAVIOR: often mostly monochrome sepia/gray/charcoal ink with ONE selective vivid emerald-green accent for a key object or detail; warmer or sacred scenes may use soft amber, gold, and muted greens with the same ink linework. Soft vignette at the parchment edges. COMPOSITION: a single unified illustration filling the frame within the parchment border, wide cinematic 16:9. Period-accurate — no cars, no power lines, no modern objects, no watermarks, no extra borders inside the art.
scenes: REAR VIEW of an old man sitting on the LEFT side of a simple wooden bench overlooking a wide Oregon valley toward the Siskiyou Mountains — we see the BACK of the bench and the back of his head, shoulders, and coat; his FACE is not visible. The RIGHT half of the bench is EMPTY. Beyond the bench: a broad valley and the dark ridgeline of the Siskiyous against a pale sky. Quiet, solitary, contemplative. Warm amber late-afternoon light, mostly monochrome sepia with soft mountain blue-gray washed out. Handwritten cursive caption: "The Siskiyous". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border with green corner flourishes.

A close-up of one open page of an old man's field notebook: the MAIN drawing is a SKETCH OF A WARBLER WING ONLY — not a whole bird — feathers of one wing carefully drawn and labeled. Below or beside it, small shaky diagrams of the warbler's BEAK SHAPE (bill profiles). Handwriting is SHAKY but legible elderly script with a tremor. Pencil and ink only, no modern pens, no whole bird body. NO PEOPLE, NO HANDS. Soft side light. Monochrome charcoal/sepia with one thin emerald accent. Caption: "Warbler notes". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border.

REAR VIEW of an elderly man walking away from the camera down a street in Grants Pass, Oregon around 1900–1910, leaning on a wooden walking stick. We see only his back — coat, hat or bare head, stick in one hand — FACE not visible. Modest wooden false-front shops, dirt or early street, boardwalks, small frontier-town buildings. Soft overcast or late light. Mostly monochrome sepia with a soft emerald-green accent on a shop awning or window. Handwritten cursive caption: "Grants Pass". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border with green corner flourishes.

FALLBACK if regenerating: same rear-view bench overlooking the Siskiyou valley as scene 1, but a translucent ghostly silhouette of a small CHILD sits on the empty right side of the bench — soft, pale, translucent, not solid, no hard face detail. Old man still on the left, backs to camera. Caption: "The empty place".

A traditional hand-weaving loom from 1850s Scotland — a wooden warp-weighted or early horizontal cottage loom with heddles, treadles, shuttle, and woven cloth on the beam, period-accurate mid-19th-century Scottish domestic weaving equipment in a plain stone or timber cottage interior. NO PEOPLE. Soft window light, dust motes, yarn and wool nearby. Warm amber and wood tones, ink linework. Handwritten cursive caption: "Scotland, 1850s". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border.

A holy sacred illustration: a dark closed wooden door that is BARELY open — only a thin crack, a few inches ajar, almost shut — yet OVERPOWERING pure gold-white light and peace pour through that narrow gap, flooding the dark room with radiance. The crack of light is thin but the light is immense. Door almost closed, NOT wide open. NO PEOPLE. Deep shadow vs sacred light. Caption: "The door". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border with green corner flourishes.

The FRONT of a closed paper envelope (NOT the back, NOT the flap side) filling most of the frame: postage stamp in the upper right corner of the FRONT, and the delivery address handwritten on the FRONT of the envelope clearly reading: Alexander Burrell Boog / Roblyn Avenue / Saint Paul, MN. Period early-1900s American letter style, cream paper, slight wear. NO people. Soft desk or table beneath. Mostly monochrome sepia with a soft green or muted stamp color. Handwritten cursive caption: "Roblyn Avenue". Storybook ink-and-watercolor on cream parchment border — show the ADDRESSED FRONT of the envelope only.
